title: AP Biology Unit 7 Question Bank v2

style_notes.easy_questions: definition-focused vocabulary checks with less application
style_notes.hard_questions: relevant distractors with minimal recycling, including partially correct College Board-style traps
style_notes.analysis_questions: clear, concrete experimental scenarios with understandable setups and interpretable data
style_notes.answer_keys: specific explanations for incorrect choices that identify the exact misconception or why the option is tempting but wrong
style_notes.carryover_policy: older valid question patterns may be kept if they still meet the updated quality standards

questions.text: Which definition best matches the term 'evolution'?
choices: change in the genetic composition of a population across generations
a pattern in which heterozygous individuals have higher fitness than either homozygote
the proportion of individuals in a population with a specific genotype
an ancestral organism shared by two or more descendant lineages
questions.explain: Evolution is defined as change in the genetic composition of a population across generations.
choice_explanations: Correct. Evolution is defined as change in the genetic composition of a population across generations.
This choice defines heterozygote advantage, not evolution. It is a real Unit 7 term, which makes it tempting, but it does not match the vocabulary word being asked about.

This choice defines common ancestor, not evolution. It is a real Unit 7 term, which makes it tempting, but it does not match the vocabulary word being asked about.

questions.text:
A population of tree frogs is being tested for Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium at a single gene.
Which condition would most directly violate the assumptions of Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?
choices: The population is in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium because the condition described affects only phenotypes and not the underlying alleles.
The population is in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium because large populations can ignore all evolutionary forces, including migration.
Several immigrants carrying allele a enter the population each generation, because gene flow introduces alleles from outside the population, so allele frequencies can change.
The population is in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium because genotype frequencies may change while allele frequencies must stay constant in any population.
questions.explain: The correct answer identifies a condition that breaks one of the Hardy-Weinberg assumptions and therefore allows allele or genotype frequencies to change.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because selection, migration, mutation, and mate choice all affect which alleles are represented in later generations. The scenario is not limited to a cosmetic phenotypic change.

This choice is partially correct because large population size does reduce the effect of drift, but it does not cancel out migration, selection, mutation, or nonrandom mating. Hardy-Weinberg requires that all assumptions be met, not just one.
Correct. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium requires no selection, no mutation, no migration, random mating, and a very large population. The condition described violates one of those assumptions.

This choice confuses genotype frequencies with allele frequencies. Under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, both allele frequencies and genotype frequencies remain stable from generation to generation if the assumptions are met.

questions.text:
In a population of 100 individuals, genotype counts at one locus are AA = 36, Aa = 48, and aa = 16.
What is the frequency of allele A?
questions.explain: The allele frequency must be computed from the number of allele copies in the population. The correct value is 0.60.
choice_explanations: This is the frequency of another genotype class, not the allele named in the prompt.
This is the frequency of one genotype class, not the frequency of the allele. A common mistake is to read AA, Aa, or aa as though genotype frequency and allele frequency were the same thing.
This is also a genotype frequency, not an allele frequency. Heterozygotes contribute one copy of each allele, so they must be counted differently.
Correct. The frequency of allele A is calculated from allele copies, not individuals: p = (2×homozygous + heterozygous) / (2×total individuals) = 0.60.

questions.text:
In a population, medium beaks crack the available seeds best.
Which evolutionary pattern best describes this situation?
choices: genetic drift
disruptive selection
directional selection
stabilizing selection
questions.explain: The scenario describes stabilizing selection because the fitness pattern depends on where phenotypes fall within the trait distribution.
choice_explanations: Drift is random change by chance. The scenario gives a nonrandom fitness advantage tied to phenotype.
Disruptive selection favors both extremes, but the scenario specifically says the middle phenotype does best.
Directional selection would favor one extreme, not the intermediate phenotype described in the prompt.
Correct. Stabilizing selection fits because medium beaks crack the available seeds best.

questions.text:
In a population, thick fur is favored during a colder climate shift.
Which evolutionary pattern best describes this situation?
choices: stabilizing selection
disruptive selection
genetic drift
directional selection
questions.explain: The scenario describes directional selection because the fitness pattern depends on where phenotypes fall within the trait distribution.
choice_explanations: Stabilizing selection favors the average phenotype, but the prompt says one end of the trait range is favored.
Disruptive selection would favor both extremes at once. The scenario instead favors a single extreme.
Drift is random. The prompt describes phenotype-dependent survival or reproduction, which is selection.
Correct. Directional selection fits because thick fur is favored during a colder climate shift.

questions.text:
In a population, very small and very large individuals use different food resources successfully.
Which evolutionary pattern best describes this situation?
choices: directional selection
disruptive selection
gene flow
stabilizing selection
questions.explain: The scenario describes disruptive selection because the fitness pattern depends on where phenotypes fall within the trait distribution.
choice_explanations: Directional selection would push the population toward one extreme, not both extremes simultaneously.
Correct. Disruptive selection fits because very small and very large individuals use different food resources successfully.
Gene flow refers to migration of alleles between populations, not differential success of multiple phenotypes within one population.
Stabilizing selection would remove the extremes, the opposite of what the scenario describes.

questions.text:
Which term best describes the following scenario?
Ten birds are blown by a storm to a remote island and start a new population there.
choices: founder effect
bottleneck effect
gene flow
genetic drift
questions.explain: The best answer is founder effect because it matches the specific mechanism described in the scenario.
choice_explanations: Correct. A small colonizing group carries only a subset of the original population's alleles, so the new population can differ from the source by chance.
A bottleneck requires a sharp drop in population size. That is not the key feature of this scenario.
Gene flow requires alleles moving between populations through migration and reproduction. This scenario is about random sampling or population size change instead.
Genetic drift is the broad category of random allele-frequency change. This distractor is tempting when the scenario is actually a more specific case such as a founder effect or bottleneck.

questions.text:
Which term best describes the following scenario?
A flood reduces a rabbit population from 8,000 to 30 survivors chosen largely by chance.
choices: founder effect
genetic drift
bottleneck effect
gene flow
questions.explain: The best answer is bottleneck effect because it matches the specific mechanism described in the scenario.
choice_explanations: The founder effect is a specific kind of drift that begins when a small group starts a new population. This scenario does not describe colonization of a new site by a few founders.
Genetic drift is the broad category of random allele-frequency change. This distractor is tempting when the scenario is actually a more specific case such as a founder effect or bottleneck.
Correct. The population size crashes, and the survivors are a nonrepresentative sample by chance, which changes allele frequencies.
Gene flow requires alleles moving between populations through migration and reproduction. This scenario is about random sampling or population size change instead.

questions.text:
Which term best describes the following scenario?
Pollen from a mainland plant population regularly fertilizes flowers in a nearby island population.
choices: bottleneck effect
founder effect
genetic drift
gene flow
questions.explain: The best answer is gene flow because it matches the specific mechanism described in the scenario.
choice_explanations: A bottleneck requires a sharp drop in population size. That is not the key feature of this scenario.
The founder effect is a specific kind of drift that begins when a small group starts a new population. This scenario does not describe colonization of a new site by a few founders.
Genetic drift is the broad category of random allele-frequency change. This distractor is tempting when the scenario is actually a more specific case such as a founder effect or bottleneck.
Correct. Movement of alleles between populations through migration and reproduction is gene flow.

questions.text:
Which term best describes the following scenario?

choices: bottleneck effect
genetic drift
gene flow
founder effect

choice_explanations: A bottleneck requires a sharp drop in population size. That is not the key feature of this scenario.

Gene flow requires alleles moving between populations through migration and reproduction. This scenario is about random sampling or population size change instead.
The founder effect is a specific kind of drift that begins when a small group starts a new population. This scenario does not describe colonization of a new site by a few founders.

Which term best describes the following scenario?
A flood reduces a rabbit population from 8,000 to 30 survivors chosen largely by chance.
choices: genetic drift
founder effect
bottleneck effect
gene flow
questions.explain: The best answer is bottleneck effect because it matches the specific mechanism described in the scenario.
choice_explanations: Genetic drift is the broad category of random allele-frequency change. This distractor is tempting when the scenario is actually a more specific case such as a founder effect or bottleneck.
The founder effect is a specific kind of drift that begins when a small group starts a new population. This scenario does not describe colonization of a new site by a few founders.
Correct. The population size crashes, and the survivors are a nonrepresentative sample by chance, which changes allele frequencies.
Gene flow requires alleles moving between populations through migration and reproduction. This scenario is about random sampling or population size change instead.

questions.text:
Which term best describes the following scenario?
Pollen from a mainland plant population regularly fertilizes flowers in a nearby island population.
choices: bottleneck effect
gene flow
founder effect
genetic drift
questions.explain: The best answer is gene flow because it matches the specific mechanism described in the scenario.
choice_explanations: A bottleneck requires a sharp drop in population size. That is not the key feature of this scenario.
Correct. Movement of alleles between populations through migration and reproduction is gene flow.
The founder effect is a specific kind of drift that begins when a small group starts a new population. This scenario does not describe colonization of a new site by a few founders.
Genetic drift is the broad category of random allele-frequency change. This distractor is tempting when the scenario is actually a more specific case such as a founder effect or bottleneck.

questions.text:
Which term best describes the following scenario?
In a tiny pond population of fish, one allele becomes common after several generations mainly by chance.
choices: gene flow
bottleneck effect
genetic drift
founder effect
questions.explain: The best answer is genetic drift because it matches the specific mechanism described in the scenario.
choice_explanations: Gene flow requires alleles moving between populations through migration and reproduction. This scenario is about random sampling or population size change instead.
A bottleneck requires a sharp drop in population size. That is not the key feature of this scenario.
Correct. Random changes in allele frequency, especially in small populations, are genetic drift.
The founder effect is a specific kind of drift that begins when a small group starts a new population. This scenario does not describe colonization of a new site by a few founders.

questions.text:
Which term best describes the following scenario?
Ten birds are blown by a storm to a remote island and start a new population there.
choices: gene flow
bottleneck effect
genetic drift
founder effect
questions.explain: The best answer is founder effect because it matches the specific mechanism described in the scenario.
choice_explanations: Gene flow requires alleles moving between populations through migration and reproduction. This scenario is about random sampling or population size change instead.
A bottleneck requires a sharp drop in population size. That is not the key feature of this scenario.
Genetic drift is the broad category of random allele-frequency change. This distractor is tempting when the scenario is actually a more specific case such as a founder effect or bottleneck.
Correct. A small colonizing group carries only a subset of the original population's alleles, so the new population can differ from the source by chance.

questions.text:
Which reproductive barrier is best illustrated by this example?
Two cricket species live in the same field, but one mates in early spring and the other mates in late summer.
choices: behavioral isolation
temporal isolation
gametic isolation
habitat isolation
questions.explain: The prompt describes temporal isolation, so that is the most precise barrier to identify.
choice_explanations: Behavioral isolation depends on mate-recognition signals such as songs, dances, or pheromones. The prompt's barrier is different.
Correct. The scenario is an example of temporal isolation.
Gametic isolation acts after mating or pollination attempts, when sperm and egg or pollen and ovule cannot unite successfully. That is not the barrier described here.
Habitat isolation means the populations occupy different habitats in the same broader area. The prompt instead points to a different barrier.

questions.text:
Which reproductive barrier is best illustrated by this example?
Two garter snake species overlap geographically, but one lives mostly in water while the other lives mainly on land.
choices: gametic isolation
behavioral isolation
temporal isolation
habitat isolation
questions.explain: The prompt describes habitat isolation, so that is the most precise barrier to identify.
choice_explanations: Gametic isolation acts after mating or pollination attempts, when sperm and egg or pollen and ovule cannot unite successfully. That is not the barrier described here.
Behavioral isolation depends on mate-recognition signals such as songs, dances, or pheromones. The prompt's barrier is different.
Temporal isolation means the populations breed at different times. This distractor is tempting only when the scenario actually states a timing difference.
Correct. The scenario is an example of habitat isolation.

questions.text:
A cladogram shows taxa W and X sharing a more recent common ancestor with each other than either does with Y or Z.
Which statement is best supported?
choices: The taxa named must have identical DNA sequences because they share a common ancestor.

questions.text:
Which conclusion is best supported by the following observation?
The forelimb bones of whales, bats, and humans have the same underlying arrangement even though they serve different functions?
choices: The observation shows that natural selection always produces identical species in every environment.
homologous structures support common ancestry
The observation can be explained only if evolution stops once a trait first appears.
The observation proves that acquired traits are inherited whenever organisms need them.
questions.explain: The strongest conclusion is the one that matches how comparative anatomy, fossils, molecules, or biogeography are used as evidence for evolution.
choice_explanations: Natural selection can produce similar adaptations, but it does not erase all historical differences or guarantee identical species in every setting.
Correct. The observation is best interpreted as evidence that homologous structures support common ancestry.
Evolution does not stop when a trait appears. Lineages continue to accumulate modifications over time.
This is Lamarckian reasoning. Need or use during an individual's lifetime does not by itself rewrite heritable DNA in the directed way this choice suggests.

questions.text:
Which statement best interprets the following evolutionary situation?
In a drought year, birds with slightly deeper beaks leave more surviving offspring than birds with shallow beaks.
choices: Fitness means the strongest or fastest organism survives longest, regardless of how many offspring it leaves.
A trait is adaptive only if it helps every individual in every environment.
The deeper-beak trait has higher fitness in that environment because carriers leave more offspring.
Any trait that looks complex must have evolved because organisms wanted or needed it.
questions.explain: The best answer uses the AP Bio meaning of fitness and adaptation rather than an everyday meaning such as strength or effort.
choice_explanations: This choice is a classic trap. In evolution, fitness is measured by relative reproductive success, not by strength, speed, or lifespan alone.
This is too absolute. Adaptiveness is environment-dependent, and many traits are beneficial only under particular conditions.
Correct. The deeper-beak trait has higher fitness in that environment because carriers leave more offspring.
This choice confuses natural selection with directed need. Selection filters heritable variation; it does not produce traits because organisms consciously need them.

questions.text:
Which statement best interprets the following evolutionary situation?
A mutation makes one lizard run faster, but the lizard produces no offspring before dying.
choices: A trait is adaptive only if it helps every individual in every environment.
Any trait that looks complex must have evolved because organisms wanted or needed it.
Fitness means the strongest or fastest organism survives longest, regardless of how many offspring it leaves.
A trait can improve performance without increasing evolutionary fitness if it does not lead to greater reproductive success.
questions.explain: The best answer uses the AP Bio meaning of fitness and adaptation rather than an everyday meaning such as strength or effort.
choice_explanations: This is too absolute. Adaptiveness is environment-dependent, and many traits are beneficial only under particular conditions.

Correct. A trait can improve performance without increasing evolutionary fitness if it does not lead to greater reproductive success.

A trait is adaptive only if it helps every individual in every environment.

questions.text:
Which process best fits this speciation scenario?
A mountain range splits one squirrel population into two isolated populations that diverge over many generations.
choices: genetic drift cannot contribute because speciation requires only natural selection
stabilizing selection keeping one species unchanged
allopatric speciation
gene flow preventing divergence
questions.explain: The correct choice identifies the mode of speciation most consistent with the presence or absence of geographic isolation and, in plants, whether polyploidy is involved.
choice_explanations: This is partially correct in recognizing that selection can matter, but it is wrong to say drift cannot contribute. Speciation can involve drift, selection, isolation, or polyploidy depending on the case.
Stabilizing selection would tend to maintain existing phenotypes rather than split populations into new species under the conditions described.
Correct. The scenario is best classified as allopatric speciation.
Gene flow generally homogenizes populations. The scenario instead describes reduced gene exchange and divergence.

questions.text:

gene flow preventing divergence
questions.explain: The correct choice identifies the mode of speciation most consistent with the presence or absence of geographic isolation and, in plants, whether polyploidy is involved.

questions.text:
Which statement best applies to this population?
Individuals prefer mates with the same coloration pattern, but no genotype has higher survival or fertility.
choices: Nonrandom mating changes only phenotype frequencies, never genotype frequencies.
Nonrandom mating can change genotype frequencies without necessarily changing allele frequencies immediately.
As long as there is no natural selection, Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium must hold exactly.
Random mating matters only for dominant alleles, not recessive alleles.
questions.explain: The key idea is that nonrandom mating breaks Hardy-Weinberg assumptions and often changes genotype proportions first.
choice_explanations: Mate choice affects who reproduces with whom, so genotype frequencies can shift even if the effect on allele frequencies is smaller or slower.
Correct. Nonrandom mating can change genotype frequencies without necessarily changing allele frequencies immediately.
This is wrong because Hardy-Weinberg requires all assumptions, including random mating. Lack of selection alone is not enough.
The random-mating assumption applies to the locus itself, not to whether an allele is dominant or recessive.

questions.text:
Which conclusion is best supported?
Species A and B differ by 2 amino acids in a conserved protein, while species A and C differ by 17 amino acids.
choices: If two sequences are similar, natural selection must have produced every nucleotide match independently.
Species A and B likely share a more recent common ancestor than species A and C.
The species with the shortest branch on a tree must be the least evolved.
Any single mutation proves one species changed directly into another living species.
questions.explain: The best answer uses molecular data the way biologists actually do: as evidence about shared ancestry, divergence, and phylogenetic relationships.
choice_explanations: Independent evolution can produce analogous phenotypes, but widespread sequence similarity across homologous genes is better explained by shared ancestry.
Correct. Species A and B likely share a more recent common ancestor than species A and C.
All living species are equally evolved in the sense that all have been evolving for the same amount of time since their last common ancestor. Branch length reflects change or time, not superiority.
Shared mutations can support common ancestry, but they do not mean one living tip species turned directly into another living tip species.

questions.text:

Two lineages show very similar DNA sequences in several homologous genes.
choices: The species with the shortest branch on a tree must be the least evolved.

If two sequences are similar, natural selection must have produced every nucleotide match independently.

questions.text:
Researchers measured beak depth in a finch population before and after a drought. During the drought, small seeds became scarce while large hard seeds remained abundant. The mean beak depth changed from 8.1 mm to 9.4 mm.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: genetic drift caused the change because any change in a trait mean must be random
the data prove that individual birds changed their beaks during life and passed that acquired change to offspring
directional selection favoring deeper beaks
gene flow is the best explanation because no selection can occur unless migrants arrive
questions.explain: The distribution of phenotypes before and after the environmental change tells you whether one extreme, the middle, or both extremes were favored.
choice_explanations: Drift is random change by chance, but the prompt links survival or reproduction to food conditions and phenotype. That pattern is evidence for selection, not random sampling alone.
This choice uses inheritance of acquired characteristics. The data are better explained by heritable variation already present in the population being filtered by selection.
Correct. The observed shift is most consistent with directional selection favoring deeper beaks.
Gene flow requires migration between populations. The study instead tracks phenotype distributions within one population under changed environmental conditions.

questions.text:
Researchers measured beak depth in a finch population over several breeding seasons. During the study, intermediate seeds were most abundant after the storm. The mean beak depth stayed near 9.0 mm, but the proportion of very small and very large beaks decreased.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: stabilizing selection around the intermediate beak depth
the data prove that individual birds changed their beaks during life and passed that acquired change to offspring
genetic drift caused the change because any change in a trait mean must be random
gene flow is the best explanation because no selection can occur unless migrants arrive
questions.explain: The distribution of phenotypes before and after the environmental change tells you whether one extreme, the middle, or both extremes were favored.
choice_explanations: Correct. The observed shift is most consistent with stabilizing selection around the intermediate beak depth.

questions.text:
Researchers measured beak depth in a finch population before and after a change in food availability. Before the change, most birds had intermediate beaks. After the change, only very small and very large seeds were common after habitat change and the population showed more birds at both the small-beak and large-beak extremes, with fewer intermediate birds.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: gene flow is the best explanation because no selection can occur unless migrants arrive
the data prove that individual birds changed their beaks during life and passed that acquired change to offspring
genetic drift caused the change because any change in a trait mean must be random
disruptive selection favoring both extremes
questions.explain: The distribution of phenotypes before and after the environmental change tells you whether one extreme, the middle, or both extremes were favored.
choice_explanations: Gene flow requires migration between populations. The study instead tracks phenotype distributions within one population under changed environmental conditions.

Correct. The observed shift is most consistent with disruptive selection favoring both extremes.

questions.text:
A population of insects was sampled before and after 10 generations of pesticide exposure. The frequency of allele A was 0.12 before exposure and 0.66 after exposure.
Which conclusion is best supported by the data?
choices: because the allele frequency changed, the population's genotype frequencies could not have changed
natural selection increased the frequency of allele A because carriers survived treatment better
the change proves that evolution occurs only when organisms deliberately adapt to a need
the pesticide must have directly caused every exposed individual to mutate into the resistant form
questions.explain: A change in allele frequency across generations is evidence of evolution. The magnitude and direction of the change help indicate whether treatment likely favored or did not favor the allele.
choice_explanations: This reverses the relationship. If allele frequencies change, genotype frequencies are expected to change as well.
Correct. natural selection increased the frequency of allele A because carriers survived treatment better.
Evolution does not require deliberate effort or need. The data show a population-level allele-frequency change, which is the AP Bio definition of evolution.
Selection acts on existing heritable variation and can also favor new mutations, but the data do not show that every exposed individual changed its own genotype in a directed way.

A population of insects was sampled before and after 10 generations of pesticide exposure. The frequency of allele R was 0.08 before exposure and 0.81 after exposure.
Which conclusion is best supported by the data?
choices: the resistance allele rose because treatment created a strong selective advantage for carriers
the pesticide must have directly caused every exposed individual to mutate into the resistant form
because the allele frequency changed, the population's genotype frequencies could not have changed
the change proves that evolution occurs only when organisms deliberately adapt to a need
questions.explain: A change in allele frequency across generations is evidence of evolution. The magnitude and direction of the change help indicate whether treatment likely favored or did not favor the allele.
choice_explanations: Correct. the resistance allele rose because treatment created a strong selective advantage for carriers.
Selection acts on existing heritable variation and can also favor new mutations, but the data do not show that every exposed individual changed its own genotype in a directed way.
This reverses the relationship. If allele frequencies change, genotype frequencies are expected to change as well.
Evolution does not require deliberate effort or need. The data show a population-level allele-frequency change, which is the AP Bio definition of evolution.

questions.text:
Biologists measured allele Q in a source population and in a nearby newly established population. In the mainland population, the frequency of allele Q was 0.70. In the island population founded by about 12 individuals, the frequency of allele Q was 0.18 in the first generation measured.
Which explanation is most consistent with these data?
choices: founder effect from a small colonizing group
gene flow from the source population must have made the two populations different
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium guarantees the frequencies should differ when a population is small
the data prove that allele Q was not present in the source population
questions.explain: When a population is started by very few individuals, its allele frequencies can differ sharply from the source simply because the founders are a random sample.
choice_explanations: Correct. The large shift at the start of a new small population is consistent with founder effect from a small colonizing group.
Gene flow usually makes populations more similar by exchanging alleles. Here, the new population begins with a different frequency because the founders were a small sample.
Hardy-Weinberg is a null model, not a rule that small populations must differ in a predictable direction. The specific shift fits random sampling of founders.
The source population frequency is given directly in the prompt, so this statement ignores the data.

Hardy-Weinberg is a null model, not a rule that small populations must differ in a predictable direction. The specific shift fits random sampling of founders.
The source population frequency is given directly in the prompt, so this statement ignores the data.
Correct. The large shift at the start of a new small population is consistent with the colonists likely carried an unusual subset of alleles by chance.

questions.text:
Biologists measured allele Q in a source population and in a nearby newly established population. In the coast population, the frequency of allele Q was 0.67. In the offshore island population founded by about 12 individuals, the frequency of allele Q was 0.29 in the first generation measured.
Which explanation is most consistent with these data?
choices: the data prove that allele Q was not present in the source population
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium guarantees the frequencies should differ when a population is small
the difference is consistent with founder effect rather than immediate adaptation alone
gene flow from the source population must have made the two populations different
questions.explain: When a population is started by very few individuals, its allele frequencies can differ sharply from the source simply because the founders are a random sample.
choice_explanations: The source population frequency is given directly in the prompt, so this statement ignores the data.
Hardy-Weinberg is a null model, not a rule that small populations must differ in a predictable direction. The specific shift fits random sampling of founders.
Correct. The large shift at the start of a new small population is consistent with the difference is consistent with founder effect rather than immediate adaptation alone.
Gene flow usually makes populations more similar by exchanging alleles. Here, the new population begins with a different frequency because the founders were a small sample.

questions.text:
Before a wildfire, 14 alleles were present at a microsatellite locus; after the wildfire, only 5 remained among survivors.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: the population could not evolve afterward because genetic drift ends once the crash is over
gene flow is the best explanation because severe mortality always increases migration
a bottleneck reduced genetic variation by chance
the data show directional selection on a trait because any reduction in variation must come from selection
questions.explain: A severe drop in population size can remove alleles by chance and reduce heterozygosity, even if the surviving individuals later reproduce normally.
choice_explanations: Drift can remain important after a crash if the population stays small, and evolution by selection or mutation can still occur later as well.
Nothing in the prompt mentions migrants entering or leaving the population. The pattern is about random loss of alleles after a population crash.
Correct. a bottleneck reduced genetic variation by chance.
Selection can reduce variation, but the prompt emphasizes survival largely by chance or across many loci, which points more directly to a bottleneck and drift.

questions.text:

Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: the population experienced a bottleneck effect
gene flow is the best explanation because severe mortality always increases migration
the data show directional selection on a trait because any reduction in variation must come from selection
the population could not evolve afterward because genetic drift ends once the crash is over

the population could not evolve afterward because genetic drift ends once the crash is over

questions.text:
In a large randomly mating population, the frequency of allele A is 0.70 and the population meets Hardy-Weinberg assumptions.
What is the expected frequency of heterozygotes?
questions.explain: Once allele frequencies are known and Hardy-Weinberg assumptions are met, the expected genotype frequencies are calculated with p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
choice_explanations: This is q², the expected frequency of homozygotes for the second allele.
This is p², the expected frequency of homozygotes for the first allele, not heterozygotes.
Correct. Under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, genotype frequencies are p², 2pq, and q².
p + q equals 1.00 for a two-allele system, but that is not a genotype frequency.

questions.text:
In a large randomly mating population, the frequency of allele B is 0.60 and the population meets Hardy-Weinberg assumptions.
What is the expected frequency of homozygotes for allele B?
questions.explain: Once allele frequencies are known and Hardy-Weinberg assumptions are met, the expected genotype frequencies are calculated with p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
choice_explanations: This is the allele frequency itself, not the genotype frequency.
Correct. Under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, genotype frequencies are p², 2pq, and q².
This is 2pq, the expected heterozygote frequency, not the homozygote frequency for the named allele.
This is q², the frequency of the opposite homozygote class.

questions.text:
A population meets Hardy-Weinberg assumptions and has allele frequencies p = 0.80 and q = 0.20.
Which expected genotype frequency is correct?
questions.explain: Once allele frequencies are known and Hardy-Weinberg assumptions are met, the expected genotype frequencies are calculated with p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
choice_explanations: This choice confuses an allele frequency with the genotype frequency p².
This uses the heterozygote label 2pq but plugs in the value of p² instead.
Correct. Under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, genotype frequencies are p², 2pq, and q².
This choice confuses the allele frequency q with the genotype frequency q².

questions.explain: Once allele frequencies are known and Hardy-Weinberg assumptions are met, the expected genotype frequencies are calculated with p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
choice_explanations: p + q equals 1.00 for a two-allele system, but that is not a genotype frequency.
This is q², the expected frequency of homozygotes for the second allele.
This is p², the expected frequency of homozygotes for the first allele, not heterozygotes.
Correct. Under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, genotype frequencies are p², 2pq, and q².

questions.text:
Population 1 had an allele frequency of 0.80 and Population 2 had an allele frequency of 0.20 at the start of the season. After many individuals from Population 2 migrated into Population 1 and reproduced, the allele frequency in Population 1 was 0.62.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium must have been restored because the recipient population changed
gene flow from the low-frequency population into the high-frequency population
genetic drift is the only explanation because migration cannot alter allele frequencies
the data prove that the allele became adaptive only after migration
questions.explain: When migrants reproduce in another population, allele frequencies often shift toward the source population's values. That convergence is the hallmark of gene flow.
choice_explanations: A change in allele frequency means one of the Hardy-Weinberg assumptions was violated, not restored. Here, migration specifically violates the no-gene-flow assumption.
Correct. gene flow from the low-frequency population into the high-frequency population.
Migration can absolutely change allele frequencies by bringing alleles into or out of populations. That process is gene flow.
The data show movement of alleles, not a direct measurement of whether the allele increased fitness.

questions.text:
Population 1 had an allele frequency of 0.15 and Population 2 had an allele frequency of 0.74 at the start of the season. After many individuals from Population 2 migrated into Population 1 and reproduced, the allele frequency in Population 1 was 0.28.

choices: the data prove that the allele became adaptive only after migration
genetic drift is the only explanation because migration cannot alter allele frequencies
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium must have been restored because the recipient population changed
gene flow moved the allele frequencies of the two populations toward each other

Population 1 had an allele frequency of 0.80 and Population 2 had an allele frequency of 0.20 at the start of the season. After many individuals from Population 2 migrated into Population 1 and reproduced, the allele frequency in Population 1 was 0.62.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium must have been restored because the recipient population changed
gene flow from the low-frequency population into the high-frequency population
the data prove that the allele became adaptive only after migration
genetic drift is the only explanation because migration cannot alter allele frequencies
questions.explain: When migrants reproduce in another population, allele frequencies often shift toward the source population's values. That convergence is the hallmark of gene flow.
choice_explanations: A change in allele frequency means one of the Hardy-Weinberg assumptions was violated, not restored. Here, migration specifically violates the no-gene-flow assumption.
Correct. gene flow from the low-frequency population into the high-frequency population.
The data show movement of alleles, not a direct measurement of whether the allele increased fitness.
Migration can absolutely change allele frequencies by bringing alleles into or out of populations. That process is gene flow.

questions.text:
Researchers tested reproduction in two populations.
Two frog populations were placed in the same pond. Males from both groups called at the same time, but females approached only the call pattern of their own population.
Which barrier is best supported by the results?
choices: reduced hybrid viability
allopatric speciation
reduced hybrid fertility
behavioral isolation
questions.explain: The key is to identify the exact stage at which reproduction fails: before mating, during fertilization, or after hybrids form.
choice_explanations: Reduced hybrid viability also requires hybrid offspring to form. The experiment instead points to a prezygotic barrier.
Allopatric speciation is a geographic pattern of divergence, not the specific reproductive barrier demonstrated by this experiment.
Reduced hybrid fertility is postzygotic and requires hybrids to form first. In the scenario, the barrier acts before successful fertilization or mating.
Correct. The experiment most directly demonstrates behavioral isolation.

questions.text:

Two iris species were hand-pollinated. Pollen from each species landed on the stigma of the other, but only same-species pollen produced pollen tubes.

reduced hybrid fertility

reduced hybrid viability

Correct. The experiment most directly demonstrates gametic isolation.
Reduced hybrid viability also requires hybrid offspring to form. The experiment instead points to a prezygotic barrier.

Correct. The experiment most directly demonstrates temporal isolation.

questions.text:
A flowering plant species normally has 2n = 14 chromosomes. After an error in meiosis, several offspring are found with 28 chromosomes and can reproduce with each other but rarely produce fertile offspring with the original population.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: the chromosome change prevents all DNA replication, so the lineage cannot persist
autopolyploid lineage formed by chromosome doubling within a species
the plants must have arisen by geographic isolation because speciation cannot happen in the same area
the plants are haploid because they have a different chromosome number from the parent population
questions.explain: A sudden doubled chromosome number in plants can create reproductive isolation quickly, especially if the new lineage is fertile with itself but not with the diploid parent population.
choice_explanations: Polyploid plants can often survive and reproduce, especially when they can pair homologous chromosomes with others of the same ploidy level.
Correct. autopolyploid lineage formed by chromosome doubling within a species.
This is a classic trap. Plant speciation can occur sympatrically through polyploidy, even without a physical barrier.
Haploid means one set of chromosomes, not simply a different chromosome number. The prompt describes chromosome doubling, which is the opposite direction.

choices: the plants are haploid because they have a different chromosome number from the parent population
autopolyploid lineage formed by chromosome doubling within a species
the plants must have arisen by geographic isolation because speciation cannot happen in the same area
the chromosome change prevents all DNA replication, so the lineage cannot persist
questions.explain: A sudden doubled chromosome number in plants can create reproductive isolation quickly, especially if the new lineage is fertile with itself but not with the diploid parent population.
choice_explanations: Haploid means one set of chromosomes, not simply a different chromosome number. The prompt describes chromosome doubling, which is the opposite direction.
Correct. autopolyploid lineage formed by chromosome doubling within a species.
This is a classic trap. Plant speciation can occur sympatrically through polyploidy, even without a physical barrier.
Polyploid plants can often survive and reproduce, especially when they can pair homologous chromosomes with others of the same ploidy level.

questions.text:
Researchers compared a homologous DNA sequence among four taxa and recorded the number of sequence differences:
A-B: 4 differences
A-C: 12 differences
A-D: 15 differences
B-C: 11 differences
B-D: 16 differences
C-D: 5 differences
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: sequence similarity cannot be used in phylogeny because only fossils provide evolutionary evidence
the pair with the most differences must be the direct ancestor-descendant pair
A and B are most closely related among the pairs listed
all taxa are equally related because they all share some common ancestor somewhere in the past
questions.explain: For homologous sequences compared over similar lengths, fewer differences usually indicate a more recent common ancestor.
choice_explanations: Molecular data are a major source of evidence in modern phylogenetics. Fossils are important too, but they are not the only valid evidence.

More differences generally indicate a more distant relationship, not a closer one. The direct ancestor-descendant wording is also a trap because living taxa are usually treated as cousins, not direct ancestors of each other.
Correct. The pair with the fewest sequence differences is inferred to share the most recent common ancestor among the choices given.
All taxa ultimately share ancestry, but relatedness is about how recent that shared ancestry is. The matrix allows closer and more distant relationships to be distinguished.

questions.text:
A moth population in a forest was sampled before and after an environmental change. At the start, 70% of moths were dark and 30% were light. After several generations, 40% were dark and 60% were light.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: because both color forms still exist, natural selection cannot be acting
the change proves individual moths switched color during life and passed that new color to offspring
directional selection favored the darker morph on soot-darkened trees
genetic drift is the only possible explanation whenever one phenotype becomes more common
questions.explain: The key is to compare phenotype frequencies before and after the environmental change and ask whether one morph was favored.
choice_explanations: Selection can change frequencies without eliminating every alternative phenotype immediately. Variation can persist even while selection acts.
The data show a population-level frequency change across generations, not a directed color change within each individual moth.
Correct. directional selection favored the darker morph on soot-darkened trees.
Drift is one possibility in some cases, but a consistent phenotype shift tied to environmental context is stronger evidence for selection.

questions.text:
A moth population in a forest was sampled before and after an environmental change. At the start, 50% of moths were dark and 50% were light. After several generations, 51% were dark and 49% were light.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: the change proves individual moths switched color during life and passed that new color to offspring
the near-equal frequencies suggest little net selection between the morphs under these conditions
genetic drift is the only possible explanation whenever one phenotype becomes more common
because both color forms still exist, natural selection cannot be acting

questions.text:
Researchers tracked morph frequencies in a population.
In a scale-eating fish, right-mouthed individuals made up 80% of the population at the start of the year. By the next year, left-mouthed fish increased from 20% to 55%.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: gene flow is the only mechanism that can keep multiple morphs in a population
the pattern rules out all selection because stable polymorphism is impossible under natural selection
the previously rare morph likely gained an advantage because prey were less prepared for it
the rare morph must have been produced because individuals needed it once the common morph became disadvantageous
questions.explain: A rare-phenotype advantage is one of the classic ways natural selection can maintain more than one phenotype in a population.
choice_explanations: Gene flow can maintain variation in some cases, but the described pattern specifically ties fitness to how common the morph is.
Selection can maintain multiple phenotypes when fitness depends on frequency. Stable or oscillating polymorphisms do not rule selection out.
Correct. the previously rare morph likely gained an advantage because prey were less prepared for it.
Need does not direct evolution. Selection acts on existing heritable variation and changes its frequency.

questions.text:
Researchers tracked morph frequencies in a population.
A rare flower-color morph received more pollinator visits than the common morph and increased over the season.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: the pattern rules out all selection because stable polymorphism is impossible under natural selection
the rare morph must have been produced because individuals needed it once the common morph became disadvantageous
negative frequency-dependent selection can maintain variation
gene flow is the only mechanism that can keep multiple morphs in a population
questions.explain: A rare-phenotype advantage is one of the classic ways natural selection can maintain more than one phenotype in a population.
choice_explanations: Selection can maintain multiple phenotypes when fitness depends on frequency. Stable or oscillating polymorphisms do not rule selection out.
Need does not direct evolution. Selection acts on existing heritable variation and changes its frequency.
Correct. negative frequency-dependent selection can maintain variation.
Gene flow can maintain variation in some cases, but the described pattern specifically ties fitness to how common the morph is.

questions.text:
Researchers tracked morph frequencies in a population.
When one mating call became very common, females began responding more often to males with the alternative rare call.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: gene flow is the only mechanism that can keep multiple morphs in a population
the pattern rules out all selection because stable polymorphism is impossible under natural selection
the fitness of the trait depends on its frequency in the population
the rare morph must have been produced because individuals needed it once the common morph became disadvantageous

choice_explanations: Gene flow can maintain variation in some cases, but the described pattern specifically ties fitness to how common the morph is.
Selection can maintain multiple phenotypes when fitness depends on frequency. Stable or oscillating polymorphisms do not rule selection out.
Correct. the fitness of the trait depends on its frequency in the population.
Need does not direct evolution. Selection acts on existing heritable variation and changes its frequency.

questions.text:
Researchers tracked morph frequencies in a population.
A prey species learned to avoid the most common warning pattern, and predators more often attacked the rarer pattern only after it became common later.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: the pattern rules out all selection because stable polymorphism is impossible under natural selection
gene flow is the only mechanism that can keep multiple morphs in a population
the rare morph must have been produced because individuals needed it once the common morph became disadvantageous
fitness changed as the phenotype's frequency changed

choice_explanations: Selection can maintain multiple phenotypes when fitness depends on frequency. Stable or oscillating polymorphisms do not rule selection out.
Gene flow can maintain variation in some cases, but the described pattern specifically ties fitness to how common the morph is.
Need does not direct evolution. Selection acts on existing heritable variation and changes its frequency.
Correct. fitness changed as the phenotype's frequency changed.

frequency-dependent selection can prevent permanent fixation of one morph
the pattern rules out all selection because stable polymorphism is impossible under natural selection

the pattern rules out all selection because stable polymorphism is impossible under natural selection
the rare morph must have been produced because individuals needed it once the common morph became disadvantageous
gene flow is the only mechanism that can keep multiple morphs in a population
questions.explain: A rare-phenotype advantage is one of the classic ways natural selection can maintain more than one phenotype in a population.
choice_explanations: Correct. frequency-dependent selection can prevent permanent fixation of one morph.
Selection can maintain multiple phenotypes when fitness depends on frequency. Stable or oscillating polymorphisms do not rule selection out.
Need does not direct evolution. Selection acts on existing heritable variation and changes its frequency.
Gene flow can maintain variation in some cases, but the described pattern specifically ties fitness to how common the morph is.

questions.text:
Researchers tracked morph frequencies in a population.
In a scale-eating fish, right-mouthed individuals made up 80% of the population at the start of the year. By the next year, left-mouthed fish increased from 20% to 55%.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: the previously rare morph likely gained an advantage because prey were less prepared for it
gene flow is the only mechanism that can keep multiple morphs in a population
the pattern rules out all selection because stable polymorphism is impossible under natural selection
the rare morph must have been produced because individuals needed it once the common morph became disadvantageous
questions.explain: A rare-phenotype advantage is one of the classic ways natural selection can maintain more than one phenotype in a population.

gene flow is the only mechanism that can keep multiple morphs in a population
the rare morph must have been produced because individuals needed it once the common morph became disadvantageous

questions.text:
Researchers tracked morph frequencies in a population.
A prey species learned to avoid the most common warning pattern, and predators more often attacked the rarer pattern only after it became common later.

choices: the pattern rules out all selection because stable polymorphism is impossible under natural selection
the rare morph must have been produced because individuals needed it once the common morph became disadvantageous
gene flow is the only mechanism that can keep multiple morphs in a population
fitness changed as the phenotype's frequency changed

choice_explanations: Selection can maintain multiple phenotypes when fitness depends on frequency. Stable or oscillating polymorphisms do not rule selection out.

questions.text:
Researchers tracked morph frequencies in a population.
Two jaw morphs oscillated in frequency over several years rather than one permanently replacing the other.

choices: gene flow is the only mechanism that can keep multiple morphs in a population
the pattern rules out all selection because stable polymorphism is impossible under natural selection
frequency-dependent selection can prevent permanent fixation of one morph
the rare morph must have been produced because individuals needed it once the common morph became disadvantageous

questions.text:
Biologists tested crosses between two closely related species.
Two plant species were crossed. F1 hybrids germinated and flowered normally, but when F1 plants were crossed with each other, most F2 seeds failed to develop.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: behavioral isolation is the best explanation because the species are already different
the result is a prezygotic barrier because fertilization occurred
hybrid breakdown
the data show that the species are actually the same species because hybrids formed at least once
questions.explain: To classify a postzygotic barrier, identify whether the hybrids fail to survive, fail to reproduce, or fail mainly in later generations.
choice_explanations: Behavioral isolation acts before mating or fertilization. Here, the cross was made and the problem appeared after zygotes formed.
This choice is self-contradictory. Once fertilization occurs, the barrier being observed is postzygotic, not prezygotic.
Correct. The outcome best matches hybrid breakdown.
The ability to form a hybrid once does not erase reproductive barriers. Sterility, inviability, or later-generation failure can still keep species distinct.

questions.text:
Biologists tested crosses between two closely related species.
Two frog species were crossed. Fertilization occurred, but most hybrid tadpoles died before metamorphosis.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: reduced hybrid viability
the data show that the species are actually the same species because hybrids formed at least once
the result is a prezygotic barrier because fertilization occurred
behavioral isolation is the best explanation because the species are already different
questions.explain: To classify a postzygotic barrier, identify whether the hybrids fail to survive, fail to reproduce, or fail mainly in later generations.
choice_explanations: Correct. The outcome best matches reduced hybrid viability.
The ability to form a hybrid once does not erase reproductive barriers. Sterility, inviability, or later-generation failure can still keep species distinct.
This choice is self-contradictory. Once fertilization occurs, the barrier being observed is postzygotic, not prezygotic.
Behavioral isolation acts before mating or fertilization. Here, the cross was made and the problem appeared after zygotes formed.
